symbols: kneeling Magi offering gifts — the nations bowing before the King of Kings
gold, frankincense, and myrrh — kingship, divinity, and sacrificial death
diverse attendants — the universality of Christ's kingship over all peoples
rocky landscape — the harsh journey of faith to reach the Savior
Man of Sorrows below in niche — the Passion prefigured even at the Nativity
description: A lunette-shaped fresco in a convent setting. At left, the Virgin Mary sits holding the infant Jesus on her lap, with Joseph standing behind. Before them, a figure kneels to present a gift. A procession of richly dressed figures including men in turbans and exotic garments extends from center to right across a rocky, mountainous landscape. Below the lunette, a small niche contains an image of the Man of Sorrows (Christ showing His wounds). The fresco is set within a whitewashed wall with a simple doorway beneath.
theological_description: Fra Angelico's fresco of the Adoration of the Magi in the convent of San Marco illustrates the Epiphany — the manifestation of Christ to the Gentile nations. Matthew 2:1-12 records that wise men from the East followed a star to Bethlehem and 'fell down and worshipped him' and 'offered him gifts: gold and frankincense and myrrh.' The diverse company represents the fulfillment of Isaiah's prophecy: 'Nations shall come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your rising... they shall bring gold and frankincense' (Isa 60:3, 6). The gifts carry theological meaning: gold for kingship, frankincense for divinity, myrrh for the burial of the mortal body. The Man of Sorrows in the niche below creates a profound typological link — even at the moment of adoration, Christ's Passion is foreshadowed. The myrrh gift already points toward His death, as does John's Gospel: 'Nicodemus brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes' for the burial (John 19:39).

scripture_refs.reason: Wise men from the East come to worship the newborn King and offer gifts
Nations shall come to your light and kings to the brightness of your rising
They shall bring gold and frankincense — the prophesied gifts
Nicodemus brings myrrh for burial — the gift of myrrh foreshadowing death
May the kings of Tarshish bring gifts — kings paying homage to the Messiah
